name: Modular Arithmetic — Clock Maths & Remainders
whatIsIt: MODULAR ARITHMETIC is 'clock maths' — you only care about the REMAINDER after dividing by a number m (the modulus). We write a ≡ b (mod m), read 'a is congruent to b mod m', meaning m divides (a − b), i.e. a and b leave the same remainder. A clock works mod 12 (after 12 you wrap to 1); days of the week work mod 7; the last digit of a number is that number mod 10.
whyNeeded: It turns huge, scary numbers into tiny remainders, which is how we find last digits, day-of-week problems, and divisibility patterns fast. It is the language of Olympiad number theory and also powers codes and computer clocks.
realLifeUses: Telling time on a 12-hour clock (13:00 shows as 1)
Working out what day of the week a date falls on (mod 7)
Finding the last digit of a big power without multiplying it all
Odometers and computer timers that reset to 0

vocabulary.meaning: The number you divide by; you keep only the remainder.
a ≡ b (mod m) means a and b have the same remainder mod m.
What is left after dividing; always from 0 to m−1.

story.title: The Clock Puzzle
story.text: Ira asks, 'If it's 9 o'clock now, what time is it 5 hours later?' Dada smiles, 'Not 14 — the clock wraps! 9 + 5 = 14, and 14 − 12 = 2, so it's 2 o'clock.' Then he asks, 'What is the remainder when 17 is divided by 5?' Ira works it out: 17 = 3×5 + 2, so 2. 'Exactly,' says Dada. 'On a mod-5 world, 17 is just 2. That's modular arithmetic — only the remainder matters.'
story.extractedProblem: On a 12-hour clock, what time is 5 hours after 9 o'clock?

lines.label: mod 12: after 11 it wraps back to 0 — the mark shows 14 ≡ 2

visual.caption: A modulus is a loop, like a clock: count past m and you wrap back to 0. Here 14 lands on 2 (mod 12).

lines.label: mod 7: days of the week loop every 7

standardMethod.summary: To compute a (mod m), divide a by m and keep the remainder (0 to m−1). Sums and products can be reduced mod m at each step to keep numbers small.
steps: Divide a by the modulus m.
The remainder r (from 0 to m−1) is a mod m; write a ≡ r (mod m).
For sums/products, you may reduce each part mod m first, then combine.
The last digit of a number is that number mod 10.

workedExamples.problem: Find the remainder when 17 is divided by 5 (17 mod 5).
What is the last digit of 7 × 7 (i.e. 49 mod 10)?

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking a mod m is the quotient, not the remainder.
commonMistakes.fix: a mod m is the REMAINDER after dividing by m (a value from 0 to m−1), not the quotient.

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting to wrap around past the modulus (writing 14 o'clock).
commonMistakes.fix: In mod m, once you reach m you loop back to 0. On a clock, 14 becomes 14 − 12 = 2.

medium.q: The last digit of 12 is 12 mod 10 = ?

challenge.q: On a 12-hour clock, 5 hours after 9 o'clock is…

questions.q: a ≡ b (mod m) means m divides…

teachBackPrompt: Use a clock face with a grown-up. Show that 5 hours after 9 o'clock is 2 (not 14) because the clock works mod 12. Then explain how 17 mod 5 = 2 means '17 is just 2 in the mod-5 world'.

revisionCard.summary: Modular arithmetic = clock maths: keep only the remainder mod m. a ≡ b (mod m) ⟺ m divides (a − b). Wrap around past m (14 o'clock → 2). Last digit = mod 10. Reduce mod m as you go to keep numbers tiny.

rememberIt.hook: Keep the remainder; wrap around like a clock (mod m)
rememberIt.unpack: a ≡ b (mod m) ⟺ m | (a − b). Reduce mod m at each step.

studentQuestions.a: It shrinks giant numbers to small remainders, so you can find last digits, weekdays and divisibility patterns without huge multiplication.

studentQuestions.q: What does the last digit tell me?
studentQuestions.a: The last digit of any number is that number mod 10 — a quick modular fact you use all the time.
